The soft_time_limit raises an exception after 50 minutes, giving the task 10 minutes to save a checkpoint before the hard_time_limit kills it at 60 minutes. At the Alliance, every distributed task must be idempotent — this is enforced by the platform team during code review and verified by integration tests that run each task twice and compare outputs.

blocks.title: Tasks must be serializable — no Python objects in arguments
blocks.content: Celery serializes task arguments to JSON (or pickle) for transmission through the broker. Passing a Polars DataFrame, a Pydantic model, or a file handle as a task argument fails at serialization time. Task arguments must be JSON-serializable primitives: strings, numbers, lists, and dictionaries. At the Alliance, the convention is: pass file paths and configuration keys as task arguments, not data. The task loads the data from the path inside the worker. This also ensures idempotency: the task reads fresh data on every execution, not stale data from a serialized argument.

Not all tasks should run on all workers. The Alliance's data residency requirements mandate that French meter validation runs on French workers (in the Paris data center), and the performance requirements demand that CPU-intensive validation tasks run on high-CPU workers while I/O-intensive Parquet reads run on high-memory workers with fast SSDs. Task routing maps tasks to queues, and queues to workers: the validate_meters task with country=fr routes to the fr-queue, which is consumed exclusively by workers in the Paris Kubernetes cluster. The routing is declared in configuration, not in code — changing a task's routing does not require modifying or redeploying the task.
Routing Configuration

from celery import Celery
from kombu import Queue

app = Celery('alliance')

# Define the queues
app.conf.task_queues = [
    Queue('ie-queue', routing_key='alliance.ie.#'),
    Queue('sc-queue', routing_key='alliance.sc.#'),
    Queue('fr-queue', routing_key='alliance.fr.#'),
    Queue('priority-queue', routing_key='alliance.priority.#'),
]

# Route tasks to queues based on their name
app.conf.task_routes = {
    'alliance.ie.*': {'queue': 'ie-queue'},
    'alliance.sc.*': {'queue': 'sc-queue'},
    'alliance.fr.*': {'queue': 'fr-queue'},
    'alliance.priority.*': {'queue': 'priority-queue'},
}

# Workers consume specific queues:
# IE workers:  celery -A alliance worker -Q ie-queue --concurrency=4
# SC workers:  celery -A alliance worker -Q sc-queue --concurrency=4
# FR workers:  celery -A alliance worker -Q fr-queue --concurrency=4
# Priority:    celery -A alliance worker -Q priority-queue --concurrency=2

# Dynamic routing for tasks that need specific worker capabilities:
@app.task(bind=True, name='alliance.ie.billing.validate_meters',
          queue='ie-queue')  # explicit queue assignment
def validate_meters(self, bronze_path: str, date: str) -> dict:
    ...

The routing configuration uses wildcard patterns: alliance.ie.* matches all Irish tasks and routes them to ie-queue. Workers are started with the -Q flag specifying which queues they consume: IE workers consume only ie-queue, ensuring that Irish tasks execute only on Irish infrastructure. The priority-queue is consumed by dedicated priority workers that handle urgent tasks (reprocessing after an incident, ad-hoc queries from the grid operations team) ahead of the nightly batch. At the Alliance, the priority queue has 2 workers with no batch tasks, guaranteeing sub-5-minute response time for urgent requests regardless of the batch processing load.
Dynamic Routing for Resource-Based Assignment

Some tasks need routing based on resource requirements rather than geography. The SCADA telemetry join (12,000 turbines, 14 GB working memory) requires a high-memory worker. The meter ID validation (5 million rows, CPU-bound) requires a high-CPU worker. Dynamic routing inspects the task's resource tags and routes to the appropriate worker pool. At the Alliance, resource-tagged routing uses a custom router function: tasks tagged with resource=high_memory route to the high-mem queue (consumed by 16 GB workers), tasks tagged with resource=gpu route to the gpu queue (consumed by GPU-equipped workers for the ML anomaly detection pipeline), and tasks without resource tags route to the default country queue. The router function is registered in the Celery configuration and evaluated at task submission time.

blocks.title: Fewer queues with clear semantics beats many queues with overlapping scope
blocks.content: The Alliance uses 7 queues: ie-queue, sc-queue, fr-queue, priority-queue, high-mem-queue, gpu-queue, and dead-letter-queue. Each queue has a single, unambiguous purpose. An early design proposed 25 queues (one per sub-region), which created monitoring complexity (25 queue depth charts instead of 7) and routing confusion (should a cross-region aggregation route to the source region's queue or the destination's?). The simplification to 7 queues reduced operational overhead without losing any routing capability because the country-level queues handle all sub-regions within a country.

The Alliance runs 12 Celery workers across 3 countries: 4 IE workers (Dublin), 4 SC workers (London), and 4 FR workers (Paris). Each worker is a Kubernetes pod running the celery worker process with prefork concurrency (4 child processes per worker, matching the 4-core pod allocation). The fleet processes approximately 200 tasks per night during the 03:00-05:00 batch window and sits idle from 05:00 to 03:00. Kubernetes Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA) scales the fleet based on queue depth: when ie-queue has more than 10 pending tasks, HPA adds an IE worker pod (up to a maximum of 8). When the queue empties, HPA scales back to the minimum of 2 workers per country.
Concurrency Models

rows: Default for all batch processing workers (validation, Silver transform)
I/O-bound tasks: API calls, webhook handlers, notifications
The API ingestion worker that processes webhook events from turbine SCADA systems
Tasks that manage their own concurrency (e.g., Polars with internal threading)
The SCADA join worker that uses Polars' internal thread pool (Polars manages its own parallelism)

blocks.content: The concurrency model matches the task's bottleneck. Prefork workers spawn child processes that bypass the GIL (Module 2 lesson 5): 4 prefork children on a 4-core pod provide true CPU parallelism for validation tasks. Eventlet workers use green threads (cooperative multitasking) for I/O-bound tasks: 100 green threads on a single core can handle 100 concurrent webhook events because each thread spends most of its time waiting for I/O (the GIL is released during I/O waits). Solo workers run one task at a time, used when the task itself manages concurrency (Polars creates its own thread pool for columnar operations and conflicts with prefork's multiprocessing). At the Alliance, 10 of 12 workers use prefork, 1 uses eventlet (the webhook ingestion worker), and 1 uses solo (the SCADA join worker).
Worker Health Monitoring

# Celery worker health check integration with Kubernetes
# The worker exposes a liveness endpoint via celery inspect ping

# Kubernetes pod spec (excerpt)
# livenessProbe:
#   exec:
#     command: ['celery', '-A', 'alliance', 'inspect', 'ping',
#               '-d', 'celery@$HOSTNAME', '--timeout', '5']
#   initialDelaySeconds: 30
#   periodSeconds: 60
#   failureThreshold: 3

# Prometheus metrics via the celery-prometheus-exporter
# Exported metrics:
# celery_workers_active            gauge    Number of active workers
# celery_task_received_total       counter  Tasks received per queue
# celery_task_succeeded_total      counter  Tasks completed successfully
# celery_task_failed_total         counter  Tasks that raised exceptions
# celery_task_runtime_seconds      histogram  Task execution time distribution
# celery_queue_length              gauge    Pending tasks per queue

# Alliance alert rules:
# - celery_queue_length{queue="ie-queue"} > 50 for 5m  -> scale up IE workers
# - celery_workers_active < 2 per country              -> worker crash alert
# - celery_task_failed_total increase > 10 in 5m       -> task failure spike

Worker health is monitored at three levels. Process-level: the Kubernetes liveness probe pings the worker every 60 seconds and restarts it after 3 failures (3 minutes of unresponsiveness). Queue-level: Prometheus metrics track queue depth and alert when pending tasks exceed the threshold, triggering HPA scale-up. Task-level: the failure rate metric alerts when more than 10 tasks fail in 5 minutes, indicating a systemic problem (a bad deployment, a broken dependency, an unavailable external service). At the Alliance, the worker fleet dashboard shows all 12 workers, their current task, their queue depth, and the task success/failure ratio — the operational view that the on-call engineer checks when a batch processing alert fires.

blocks.title: Prefork workers and heavy library imports do not mix well
blocks.content: Prefork workers fork the parent process to create child processes. If the parent has already imported pyarrow (800 ms of initialization, 200 MB of memory), each child inherits the import but also inherits the parent's memory pages (copy-on-write). On Linux, this works efficiently until a child modifies the imported module's state (triggering copy-on-write page duplication). At the Alliance, the worker startup uses Module 2 lesson 8's lazy import pattern: the parent process imports only Celery and structlog, and each child imports pyarrow and Polars on first task execution. This reduces the parent's memory from 400 MB to 80 MB and avoids copy-on-write duplication of pyarrow's C++ allocator state.

sections.title: Distributed Task DAGs with Celery Canvas

blocks.content: Module 4 mid-level built single-server pipeline DAGs: read → validate → transform → write, executed sequentially within one process. Distributed DAGs compose tasks across workers: read_meters on Worker 1 and read_weather on Worker 2 execute in parallel, and enrich_with_weather on Worker 3 executes only after both reads complete. Celery Canvas provides the primitives for composing distributed DAGs: chain (sequential), group (parallel), chord (parallel + callback), and combinations thereof. The Canvas primitives map directly to the pipeline DAG patterns from Module 4 mid-level but execute across a worker fleet instead of within a single process.
The Canvas Primitives

from celery import chain, group, chord

# chain: sequential execution across workers
# read -> validate -> transform -> write
pipeline = chain(
    read_bronze_meters.s(bronze_path='/data/ie/bronze/date=2025-01-15'),
    validate_meters.s(),       # receives read result as first arg
    transform_to_silver.s(),   # receives validate result as first arg
    write_silver.s(silver_path='/data/ie/silver'),
)
result = pipeline.apply_async(queue='ie-queue')

# group: parallel execution, results collected as a list
parallel_reads = group(
    read_bronze_meters.s(path=f'/data/ie/bronze/date=2025-01-{d:02d}')
    for d in range(1, 16)  # 15 days in parallel
)
results = parallel_reads.apply_async()

# chord: parallel execution + callback when all complete
enrichment_dag = chord(
    # Header: parallel reads
    group(
        read_bronze_meters.s('/data/ie/bronze/date=2025-01-15'),
        read_weather_data.s('/data/ie/weather/date=2025-01-15'),
        read_price_data.s('/data/ie/prices/date=2025-01-15'),
    ),
    # Callback: runs after all header tasks complete
    enrich_and_write_silver.s(date='2025-01-15'),
)
enrichment_dag.apply_async()

The .s() method creates a signature (a serializable task reference with arguments) that can be composed into Canvas workflows. The chain passes each task's return value as the first argument to the next task. The group executes all tasks in parallel and collects results as a list. The chord combines parallel execution with a callback: the 3 reads execute in parallel on 3 workers, and enrich_and_write_silver executes on a fourth worker after all 3 reads complete. At the Alliance, the nightly enrichment pipeline uses a chord: read_meters, read_weather, and read_prices execute in parallel (saving 45 seconds of sequential I/O), and the enrichment step joins the three datasets after all reads complete.
Composing Complex DAGs

Canvas primitives compose: a chain of chords creates a multi-stage pipeline where each stage has parallel tasks with a synchronization point. The Alliance's full Silver pipeline is chain(chord([read_meters, read_weather, read_prices], validate_and_enrich), transform_to_silver, write_silver) — a two-stage pipeline where stage 1 reads 3 sources in parallel and validates, and stage 2 transforms and writes sequentially. The DAG structure is defined in YAML (extending Module 4 mid-level's YAML pipeline definitions) and converted to Canvas objects at runtime. At the Alliance, 12 of the 35 distributed pipelines use Canvas DAGs; the remaining 23 are simple single-task pipelines (one task per date partition, no inter-task dependencies) that use Celery's basic task.delay() without Canvas.

blocks.title: Chords with many header tasks can overwhelm the result backend
blocks.content: A chord with 365 header tasks (one per day for annual reprocessing) stores 365 intermediate results in Redis before the callback executes. If each result is 10 KB, the chord consumes 3.6 MB of Redis memory — manageable. But if each result is 10 MB (a large validation report), the chord consumes 3.6 GB. At the Alliance, the rule is: chord header tasks must return lightweight results (counts, paths, status codes), not data. If the header task produces large output, it writes to storage (Parquet, S3) and returns the path. The callback reads from the path. This pattern caps chord Redis usage at approximately 1 KB per header task regardless of data volume.

A single-server pipeline fails in one way: an exception propagates up the call stack, the pipeline exits, and the operator restarts it. A distributed pipeline fails in seven additional ways: a task serialization error (the argument is not JSON-serializable), a broker connection loss (the task is submitted but never reaches a worker), a worker crash mid-task (the task disappears from the queue and never produces a result), a result backend failure (the task succeeds but the result is lost), a timeout (the task runs longer than the soft or hard limit), a deserialization error (the result cannot be unpickled), and a chord synchronization failure (one header task fails and the callback never fires). Each failure requires a different handling strategy.
The Retry Policy

from celery import Task
from celery.exceptions import SoftTimeLimitExceeded
import structlog

logger = structlog.get_logger()

class AllianceTask(Task):
    """Base task with Alliance retry and error handling policies."""
    autoretry_for = (ConnectionError, TimeoutError, OSError)
    retry_backoff = True          # exponential backoff: 1s, 2s, 4s, 8s, ...
    retry_backoff_max = 300       # cap at 5 minutes
    max_retries = 3               # give up after 3 retries
    retry_jitter = True           # randomize backoff to prevent thundering herd

    def on_failure(self, exc, task_id, args, kwargs, einfo):
        """Called when all retries are exhausted."""
        logger.error('task_permanently_failed',
                     task_id=task_id, task_name=self.name,
                     error=str(exc), error_type=type(exc).__name__,
                     retries=self.request.retries)
        # Route to dead letter queue for manual investigation
        route_to_dead_letter(task_id=task_id, task_name=self.name,
                             args=args, kwargs=kwargs, error=str(exc))

    def on_retry(self, exc, task_id, args, kwargs, einfo):
        logger.warning('task_retrying',
                       task_id=task_id, retry=self.request.retries,
                       max_retries=self.max_retries, error=str(exc))

@app.task(base=AllianceTask, bind=True, name='alliance.ie.billing.validate_meters')
def validate_meters(self, bronze_path: str, date: str) -> dict:
    try:
        # ... task logic ...
        return {'valid': len(valid), 'rejected': len(rejected)}
    except SoftTimeLimitExceeded:
        logger.warning('task_timeout', task_id=self.request.id, date=date)
        save_checkpoint(date)  # save progress before hard kill
        raise  # re-raise to trigger retry

The AllianceTask base class encapsulates the retry policy: retry transient errors (ConnectionError, TimeoutError) with exponential backoff (1, 2, 4, 8 seconds) up to 3 attempts. If all retries fail, on_failure routes the task to the dead letter queue — a dedicated queue where permanently failed tasks are stored for manual investigation. The dead letter queue is not consumed by any worker; it is a holding area that the on-call engineer reviews during the morning triage. At the Alliance, the dead letter queue processes approximately 2-5 tasks per week (out of 1,400 tasks per week), with the most common cause being external API unavailability that exceeds the 3-retry budget.
Idempotency as the Foundation of Retry Safety

The retry policy works only because every Alliance task is idempotent (Module 10 mid-level). If validate_meters writes Silver data using the append pattern, a retry after a partial write produces duplicate rows. Because the task uses the overwrite pattern (write to a temporary directory, atomically rename), a retry replaces any partial output from the failed attempt with a complete output from the successful retry. The idempotency requirement is not a preference — it is a structural prerequisite for distributed task execution. At the Alliance, the CI quality gates (Module 7 mid-level) include an idempotency test for every distributed task: the test runs the task twice with the same arguments and asserts that the output is identical.

blocks.title: The dead letter queue is the most important queue in the system
blocks.content: Tasks in the dead letter queue represent permanent failures that automatic retries could not resolve. Each task is a small incident: data that was not processed, a pipeline that did not complete, a consumer that may receive stale data. At the Alliance, the dead letter queue is reviewed daily at 08:00 (after the nightly batch completes). Each task in the queue is either resubmitted with a fix (the external API is back up), escalated (the failure indicates a systemic problem), or acknowledged (the failure is expected and the data will be processed in the next scheduled run). A dead letter queue with more than 10 tasks triggers an incident response.

Every Celery task produces a result: the return value of the task function, serialized to JSON and stored in the result backend (Redis at the Alliance). The result is identified by the task ID and can be retrieved by the client with result.get(). For simple pipelines (submit task, wait for result), result management is straightforward. For Canvas DAGs (chord callbacks that depend on header task results, chains that pass results between tasks), result management becomes the coordination mechanism — and result backend failures become pipeline failures. The result backend is a critical-path dependency: if Redis is down, chord callbacks cannot fire, chain tasks cannot receive their inputs, and the entire distributed pipeline stalls.
Result Expiry and Cleanup

# Result backend configuration
app.conf.update(
    result_backend='redis://results.alliance.internal:6379/1',
    result_expires=3600,       # results expire after 1 hour
    result_extended=True,      # store task metadata (name, args, start time)
    result_compression='zlib', # compress large results to save Redis memory
)

# Monitoring result backend memory usage
# Alliance Redis result backend:
# - 1,400 tasks/week * 1 KB avg result = 1.4 MB/week
# - With 1-hour TTL: max ~60 results * 1 KB = 60 KB active at peak
# - With no TTL: 1.4 MB/week accumulating indefinitely -> 73 MB/year

# The 1-hour TTL is critical: without it, results accumulate
# and Redis memory grows linearly until the server runs out of memory.

# For tasks where results are not needed (fire-and-forget):
@app.task(ignore_result=True, name='alliance.ie.billing.send_notification')
def send_billing_notification(team: str, date: str) -> None:
    """Fire-and-forget: no result stored, no Redis memory consumed."""
    send_slack_message(channel=f'#{team}-alerts', message=f'Billing data ready for {date}')

The result_expires setting is the single most important result backend configuration. Without it, results accumulate indefinitely: 1,400 results per week at 1 KB each grows to 73 MB per year. With 100 KB average results (validation reports with rejection breakdowns), the growth is 7.3 GB per year — enough to exhaust a default Redis instance. At the Alliance, the 1-hour TTL ensures that results are available for the chord callback and the morning triage review but are cleaned up before the next night's batch. Fire-and-forget tasks (notifications, audit log entries) use ignore_result=True to skip the result backend entirely.
Result Backend High Availability

A Redis restart during chord execution causes all in-flight chords to fail: the header results are lost, the callback cannot fire, and the pipeline stalls. At the Alliance, the result backend uses Redis Sentinel with automatic failover: 3 Redis instances (one primary, two replicas) with Sentinel monitoring. If the primary fails, Sentinel promotes a replica within 10 seconds. Celery reconnects to the new primary automatically. The 10-second failover window may cause a few tasks to fail (their results are not replicated to the new primary), but the retry policy (lesson 6) resubmits them. The alternative — using PostgreSQL as the result backend — provides durability (results survive restarts) but adds 5-10 ms of latency per result write, which the Alliance determined was unnecessary given the Sentinel failover strategy.

blocks.title: Use S3 as the result backend for large results, Redis for metadata
blocks.content: If a task produces a 50 MB validation report as its result, storing it in Redis consumes 50 MB of memory per result — unsustainable at scale. At the Alliance, the pattern is: the task writes large outputs to S3 (or the local file system) and returns a lightweight result (the S3 path, row counts, status) to Redis. The chord callback reads the S3 path from the Redis result and fetches the full output from S3. This pattern caps Redis memory usage at approximately 1 KB per result regardless of output size, while preserving the full output in durable storage.

Module 9 mid-level built the observability stack for single-server pipelines: structlog for logs, Prometheus for metrics, and health checks for Kubernetes probes. Distributed pipelines add three monitoring dimensions that single-server pipelines do not have. First, worker-level visibility: which workers are processing tasks, which are idle, and which have crashed. Second, queue-level visibility: how many tasks are pending in each queue, how fast are they being consumed, and is any queue growing faster than workers can drain it. Third, distributed tracing: following a single pipeline run across multiple workers, correlating the read task on Worker 1 with the enrichment task on Worker 3 to reconstruct the end-to-end timeline.
Distributed Tracing with OpenTelemetry

from opentelemetry import trace
from opentelemetry.sdk.trace import TracerProvider
from opentelemetry.sdk.trace.export import BatchSpanExporter
from opentelemetry.exporter.otlp.proto.grpc.trace_exporter import OTLPSpanExporter
import structlog

logger = structlog.get_logger()
tracer = trace.get_tracer('alliance.pipeline')

def submit_enrichment_pipeline(date: str) -> str:
    """Submit the enrichment DAG with a distributed trace."""
    with tracer.start_as_current_span('enrichment_pipeline') as span:
        trace_id = format(span.get_span_context().trace_id, '032x')
        span.set_attribute('pipeline.date', date)
        span.set_attribute('pipeline.country', 'ie')

        # Pass trace context to distributed tasks via headers
        enrichment_dag = chord(
            group(
                read_meters.s(path=f'/data/ie/bronze/date={date}'),
                read_weather.s(path=f'/data/ie/weather/date={date}'),
            ),
            enrich_and_write.s(date=date),
        )
        result = enrichment_dag.apply_async(
            headers={'trace_id': trace_id},  # propagate trace context
        )
        logger.info('pipeline_submitted', trace_id=trace_id, date=date)
        return trace_id

OpenTelemetry assigns a trace ID to the pipeline submission and propagates it to every distributed task via Celery message headers. Each task creates a child span under the parent trace, recording its start time, duration, success/failure, and custom attributes (row count, rejection rate). The trace collector (Jaeger or Grafana Tempo) assembles the spans into a timeline view: the enrichment_pipeline span contains child spans for read_meters (Worker 1, 12 seconds), read_weather (Worker 2, 8 seconds), and enrich_and_write (Worker 3, 15 seconds). The timeline reveals that read_meters and read_weather execute in parallel (overlapping spans) and that enrich_and_write starts only after both reads complete (sequential after the chord header).
The Worker Fleet Dashboard

The Alliance's worker fleet dashboard (Grafana) has four panels. Worker status: a table of all 12 workers showing their current state (active, idle, offline), current task, and uptime. Queue depth: a time-series chart of pending tasks per queue (ie-queue, sc-queue, fr-queue, priority-queue) showing the nightly processing wave (tasks queued at 03:00, drained by 04:30). Task throughput: tasks completed per minute per country, showing whether workers are keeping up with the submission rate. Error rate: failed tasks per minute with drill-down to the error type (timeout, connection error, validation error). The dashboard is the first screen the on-call engineer opens when a batch processing alert fires.

blocks.title: Distributed tracing is the correlation ID from Module 9 extended across processes
blocks.content: Module 9 mid-level's correlation ID (run_id) tracked a pipeline run within a single process. OpenTelemetry's trace ID tracks a pipeline run across multiple processes on multiple servers. The engineering pattern is identical: bind an identifier at the start, propagate it through the execution chain, and use it to correlate all events for a single run. The difference is scope: run_id propagates through a call stack (structlog contextvars); trace_id propagates through a message queue (Celery headers) and a collector (Jaeger/Tempo).

Distribution adds infrastructure (a broker, a result backend, a trace collector), operational complexity (worker fleet monitoring, queue depth management, dead letter triage), and latency (task serialization, broker round-trip, result retrieval). A task that takes 5 seconds on a single server takes 6.5 seconds when distributed (0.5 seconds serialization, 0.2 seconds broker, 0.3 seconds result, 0.5 seconds deserialization). Distribution is justified when the benefit (parallelism, data residency, memory isolation) exceeds this overhead. For 165 of the Alliance's 200+ pipelines, it does not: they process sub-regional data that fits on a single server and completes within the SLA without distribution.
The Distribution Decision Framework

rows: Runtime > 80% of SLA on the target server specification
PII data that must stay within a specific jurisdiction
Peak memory > 80% of server memory with chunked processing
The pipeline consists of independent partitions
More than 8 independent partitions that can run in parallel
Do NOT distribute — run on a single server
Single-server runtime < 50% of SLA and memory < 50% of allocation

blocks.content: The framework uses the 80% threshold as the trigger: a pipeline that uses 80% of the SLA or 80% of the memory allocation will breach the limit within 1-2 years at the Alliance's 15% annual growth rate. Distributing at 80% provides a buffer for growth without requiring an urgent migration under pressure. Conversely, distributing a pipeline at 30% of SLA is premature: the operational complexity of distribution is unjustified when the single-server headroom is comfortable. At the Alliance, the quarterly capacity planning review (Module 10 mid-level, lesson 9) identifies pipelines approaching the 80% threshold and schedules their distribution migration for the following quarter.
The 35/165 Split

Of the Alliance's 200+ pipelines, 35 are distributed and 165+ run on single servers. The distributed pipelines handle the 3 use cases that require distribution: 25 pipelines are distributed for data residency (they process cross-country data and must route tasks to country-specific workers), 7 pipelines are distributed for CPU parallelism (they process more data than a single server can validate within the SLA), and 3 pipelines are distributed for memory isolation (the SCADA join requires 14 GB, which exceeds the standard 8 GB pod). The remaining 165+ pipelines run on single servers with the mid-level concurrent patterns (ThreadPoolExecutor for I/O, ProcessPoolExecutor for CPU) because they complete within their SLAs on a single 4-core, 8 GB pod.

blocks.title: Distributing a simple pipeline is engineering malpractice
blocks.content: A pipeline that reads one CSV file, validates 50,000 rows, and writes one Parquet file completes in 3 seconds on a single server. Distributing it across 4 workers adds 2 seconds of overhead (serialization + broker + result), produces the same output in 2.5 seconds of distributed execution (barely faster than sequential), and adds 4 failure modes (broker, workers, result backend, network). The net result: 4.5 seconds distributed vs 3 seconds local, with 4x the operational complexity. At the Alliance, the platform review board rejected 3 distribution proposals in the first year because the pipelines did not meet the 80% threshold. Each rejection saved approximately 20 hours of migration effort and ongoing operational overhead.

sections.title: The Atlantic Energy Alliance Task Architecture

blocks.content: This module designed the distributed task architecture that scales the Alliance's processing beyond single-server limits: Celery's broker-worker-backend architecture for decoupled task execution (lesson 2), country-specific and resource-based routing for data residency compliance and workload optimization (lesson 3), worker fleet management with autoscaling and concurrency model selection (lesson 4), Canvas primitives for distributed DAGs with parallel reads and synchronization points (lesson 5), the retry policy with dead letter routing for distributed error handling (lesson 6), result management with TTL and S3 offloading for backend sustainability (lesson 7), and distributed tracing with OpenTelemetry for cross-worker pipeline visibility (lesson 8).
The Task Architecture Summary

rows: Redis (3-node Sentinel) with 4 country queues + priority + high-mem + DLQ
Redis provides sub-millisecond enqueue; Sentinel provides failover in 10 seconds
12 workers (4 IE, 4 SC, 4 FR), prefork with 4 processes each, HPA scaling 2-8 per country
Country-specific workers for data residency; HPA scales to nightly batch load
Redis with 1-hour TTL, S3 for large outputs, ignore_result for fire-and-forget
1-hour TTL prevents memory accumulation; S3 offloads large results
Canvas: chord for parallel reads + enrichment callback, chain for sequential steps
12 distributed pipelines use Canvas DAGs; 23 use simple task.delay()
AllianceTask base: 3 retries, exponential backoff, DLQ routing on permanent failure
Transient errors retry automatically; permanent failures are triaged daily at 08:00
Flower + Prometheus + OpenTelemetry traces + Grafana worker fleet dashboard
Worker-level, queue-level, and trace-level visibility for the on-call engineer

blocks.content: The scaling patterns phase (Modules 1-3) is complete. Module 1 designed the organizational architecture: namespaces, ownership, repository strategy, and deployment topology. Module 2 went inside the CPython runtime: memory allocators, garbage collection, GIL boundaries, and C extension integration. Module 3 designed the distributed processing architecture: task routing, worker fleets, Canvas DAGs, and the operational patterns for a multi-country worker fleet. Together, the three modules answer the scaling question: how does a Python data platform grow from 3 teams and 12 pipelines to 10 teams and 200+ pipelines? The answer: organizational conventions (Module 1), runtime optimization (Module 2), and horizontal distribution (Module 3) — applied selectively based on each pipeline's actual needs.

blocks.title: What comes next
blocks.content: Module 4 shifts from internal scaling to external interfaces: how do the 10 teams consume data products from each other without direct pipeline coupling? The API-first data product architecture uses GraphQL for typed query interfaces, webhooks for event-driven ingestion, and streaming HTTP for real-time feeds. The distributed task architecture from this module provides the execution layer: a GraphQL query that triggers a data product refresh submits a Celery task to the appropriate country queue, and the result is served through the API after the task completes. Module 4 builds the consumption interface; Module 3 provides the execution engine behind it.

modules.title: API-First Data Product Architecture
modules.description: Design API-first data product interfaces — GraphQL for typed queries, webhook handlers for event-driven ingestion, streaming HTTP for real-time feeds, and the versioning strategies that let 10 consuming teams evolve independently.

blocks.content: At mid-level scale, teams consumed Silver Parquet files directly: the billing team mounted the /data/ie/silver directory, read the Parquet files with pyarrow, and processed them in their own pipeline. This worked because 3 teams shared one server and one file system. At Alliance scale, 10 teams across 3 countries cannot mount the same directory. The Scottish billing team needs Irish meter data for cross-border grid balancing, but the Irish Silver directory is on the Dublin Kubernetes cluster — not accessible from London. The team could copy the Parquet files to S3 and share the bucket, but then 6 consumers read the same files in 6 different ways, none of them knowing whether the file is fresh, what schema version it uses, or who to contact when it breaks.
Files vs Data Products

left.label: File-Based Consumption
points: Consumer reads Parquet directly. Must know the file path, the schema, the partition format, and the freshness schedule.
Access control is file-system permissions. Granting read access exposes the entire directory, not specific columns or rows.
Schema changes break consumers silently. A renamed column produces an empty result, not an error message.
No usage metrics. The producer cannot know which teams consume the data or which columns they use.

right.label: Data Product Consumption
points: Consumer calls an API. The API abstracts the storage format, enforces the schema, and guarantees freshness via the SLA.
Access control is API-level: per-team authentication, per-field authorization, and rate limiting per consumer.
Schema changes are versioned. The API serves v1 and v2 simultaneously during the migration window.
Every API call is logged. The producer knows the consumer, the fields requested, the query latency, and the access frequency.

blocks.content: A data product is a dataset with an API, an SLA, an owner, and a schema contract. The API is the consumption interface that replaces direct file access. The SLA guarantees freshness and availability (Module 10 mid-level). The owner is accountable for the product's quality (Module 1 lesson 5). The schema contract defines the fields, types, and compatibility rules (Module 5 senior, upcoming). This module designs the API layer — the interface through which 10 teams consume data products without knowing or caring about the underlying Parquet files, Delta tables, or Celery tasks that produce them.

blocks.title: The API is the product boundary
blocks.content: Behind the API, the producer can change the storage format (Parquet to Delta Lake), the processing engine (single-server to distributed Celery), or the refresh schedule (daily to hourly) without affecting consumers. The API contract is the stable surface; everything behind it is implementation. This decoupling is what makes data products composable: the Scottish billing team depends on the Irish meter API's contract, not on the Dublin cluster's file system layout. When the Irish team migrates from Parquet to Delta Lake (Module 7 senior), the Scottish team's code does not change.

sections.title: GraphQL for Typed Data Product APIs

blocks.content: REST APIs return fixed response shapes: GET /meters/IE-DUB-N-00142 returns all 15 fields for a meter, whether the consumer needs 3 or 15. The Scottish billing team needs meter_id, daily_kw, and date — 3 fields. The analytics team needs all 15 fields plus derived aggregations. With REST, both receive the same response, and the billing team discards 80% of the payload. GraphQL solves this with consumer-driven queries: the consumer specifies exactly which fields it needs, and the API returns only those fields. This reduces network transfer, eliminates over-fetching, and makes the consumer's dependency on specific fields explicit in the query — enabling impact analysis when fields change.
The GraphQL Schema with Strawberry

import strawberry
from datetime import date

@strawberry.type
class MeterReading:
    """A single meter reading from the Silver layer."""
    meter_id: str
    daily_kw: float
    date: date
    region: str
    customer_type: str
    daily_mwh: float                  # derived field
    tariff_category: str | None = None  # optional: only for billing consumers

@strawberry.type
class MeterReadingsPage:
    """Paginated response for meter reading queries."""
    readings: list[MeterReading]
    total_count: int
    has_next_page: bool
    cursor: str | None = None

@strawberry.type
class Query:
    @strawberry.field
    def meter_readings(
        self,
        region: str | None = None,
        date_from: date | None = None,
        date_to: date | None = None,
        customer_type: str | None = None,
        first: int = 100,
        after: str | None = None,
    ) -> MeterReadingsPage:
        """Query meter readings with filtering and pagination."""
        # Resolver reads from Silver Parquet/Delta with predicate pushdown
        filters = build_filters(region=region, date_from=date_from,
                                date_to=date_to, customer_type=customer_type)
        readings, total = query_silver_meters(filters, limit=first, cursor=after)
        return MeterReadingsPage(
            readings=readings, total_count=total,
            has_next_page=len(readings) == first,
            cursor=readings[-1].meter_id if readings else None,
        )

schema = strawberry.Schema(query=Query)

The Strawberry library maps Python type annotations directly to GraphQL schema types: MeterReading becomes a GraphQL object type with typed fields, and the meter_readings resolver becomes a queryable field with typed arguments. The consumer queries only the fields it needs: query { meterReadings(region: "DUBLIN-NORTH") { meterId, dailyKw } } returns a JSON response with only those 2 fields — not all 7. The resolver translates GraphQL filters into predicate pushdown on the underlying Parquet or Delta Lake storage, reading only the relevant partitions and columns. At the Alliance, the meter readings API serves 6 consuming teams with different query patterns, all using the same schema but requesting different field subsets.
Why GraphQL Over REST for Data Products

REST requires the API designer to predict every consumer's query pattern and create endpoints accordingly: /meters/by-region, /meters/by-date-range, /meters/by-customer-type, /meters/by-region-and-date. With 10 consuming teams making different queries, the endpoint count grows combinatorially. GraphQL provides a single endpoint where the consumer constructs the query, and the resolver handles filtering and field selection dynamically. The trade-off: GraphQL queries are more complex to cache (each query is unique), more complex to rate-limit (the cost depends on the query's field depth), and require the consumer to learn the GraphQL query language. At the Alliance, GraphQL is used for data product APIs (complex, varied queries from many consumers); REST is used for operational APIs (simple, predictable endpoints like /healthz and /metrics).

blocks.title: GraphQL without depth limiting is a denial-of-service vector
blocks.content: A GraphQL query can request deeply nested fields that trigger expensive joins: query { meterReadings { region { turbines { scadaReadings { ... } } } } }. Without depth limiting, a single query can consume minutes of server compute. At the Alliance, every GraphQL endpoint enforces a maximum query depth of 3, a maximum field count of 50, and a query cost budget of 1000 points (each field costs 1 point, each nested relation costs 10 points). Queries exceeding these limits return a 400 error with a description of which limit was exceeded.

The mid-level pipeline polled external systems on a schedule: every night at 03:00, the pipeline called the EPEX SPOT API to fetch the latest energy prices. Polling works for data sources that update on a predictable schedule. The Alliance's SCADA system does not update on a schedule — it pushes turbine status changes as they happen: a turbine going offline, a power output spike, a maintenance mode activation. These events arrive at unpredictable times and must be ingested within seconds for the grid operations team to respond. Webhook ingestion inverts the data flow: instead of the pipeline pulling data from the source, the source pushes data to the pipeline via an HTTP POST callback.
The Webhook Endpoint

from fastapi import FastAPI, Request, HTTPException, Depends
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
from datetime import datetime
import hashlib
import hmac
import structlog

logger = structlog.get_logger()
app = FastAPI()

class TurbineEvent(BaseModel):
    """SCADA turbine status event."""
    turbine_id: str
    event_type: str = Field(pattern='^(status_change|power_spike|maintenance)$')
    timestamp: datetime
    power_output_kw: float
    wind_speed_ms: float
    event_id: str  # idempotency key from the source system

def verify_webhook_signature(request: Request) -> None:
    """Verify the HMAC signature to authenticate the SCADA system."""
    signature = request.headers.get('X-Webhook-Signature', '')
    body = await request.body()
    expected = hmac.new(WEBHOOK_SECRET, body, hashlib.sha256).hexdigest()
    if not hmac.compare_digest(signature, f'sha256={expected}'):
        raise HTTPException(status_code=401, detail='Invalid webhook signature')

@app.post('/webhooks/scada/turbine-events')
async def receive_turbine_event(
    event: TurbineEvent,
    _auth: None = Depends(verify_webhook_signature),
) -> dict:
    """Receive and process a SCADA turbine event."""
    # Idempotency: check if this event_id was already processed
    if await event_already_processed(event.event_id):
        logger.info('duplicate_event_skipped', event_id=event.event_id)
        return {'status': 'already_processed'}

    # Submit to Celery for async processing
    process_turbine_event.delay(event.model_dump(mode='json'))
    await mark_event_processed(event.event_id)

    logger.info('webhook_received', event_id=event.event_id,
                turbine=event.turbine_id, event_type=event.event_type)
    return {'status': 'accepted'}

The webhook endpoint validates the request (HMAC signature verification ensures only the authenticated SCADA system can submit events), validates the payload (the Pydantic model enforces typed fields and constrained values), checks for duplicate delivery (the event_id provides idempotent processing — the same event submitted twice is processed once), and submits the event to Celery for asynchronous processing. The endpoint returns immediately after queuing the task, so the SCADA system's HTTP timeout is not blocked by the processing time. At the Alliance, the SCADA webhook receives approximately 2,000 events per hour during normal operations and up to 15,000 per hour during storm conditions when multiple turbines change status simultaneously.
At-Least-Once Delivery and Idempotent Processing

Webhook delivery is inherently at-least-once: if the Alliance's endpoint returns a 500 error (or the network drops the response), the SCADA system retries the POST. The event arrives twice. Without idempotency checking, the pipeline processes the event twice — potentially recording a turbine status change as two separate events in the timeline. The event_id-based deduplication (lesson 2's idempotency pattern extended to webhook ingestion) prevents this: the first delivery processes the event and records the event_id in a deduplication store (Redis with 24-hour TTL). The second delivery checks the store, finds the event_id, and skips processing. At the Alliance, approximately 0.3% of webhook deliveries are duplicates — small in percentage but significant at 2,000 events per hour (6 duplicate events per hour that would corrupt the timeline without deduplication).

blocks.title: Return 200 before processing, not after
blocks.content: The webhook endpoint must return 200 within the source system's timeout (typically 5-10 seconds). If the endpoint processes the event synchronously and the processing takes 15 seconds, the source system times out, considers the delivery failed, and retries — creating unnecessary duplicate deliveries. At the Alliance, the endpoint validates, deduplicates, and queues the event to Celery in approximately 50 milliseconds, returning 200 before any processing begins. The Celery worker handles the actual processing asynchronously, and any processing failure is handled by the Celery retry policy (Module 3 lesson 6), not by the webhook retry.

The grid operations team monitors turbine output in real-time: a dashboard showing the current power output, status, and wind speed for all 12,000 turbines across the Alliance. Polling the GraphQL API every 5 seconds creates 720 requests per minute — and each response returns the full turbine dataset even when only 3 turbines have changed since the last poll. Server-Sent Events (SSE) provides a more efficient pattern: the client opens a single long-lived HTTP connection, and the server pushes updates as they occur. When turbine IE-GAL-C-042 changes from 'online' to 'maintenance', only that turbine's update is pushed — not the full dataset. The dashboard receives the update within 1 second of the status change.
The SSE Endpoint

from fastapi import FastAPI
from fastapi.responses import StreamingResponse
import asyncio
import json
import structlog

logger = structlog.get_logger()
app = FastAPI()

async def turbine_event_stream(country: str | None = None):
    """Generate Server-Sent Events for turbine status changes."""
    subscriber = await subscribe_to_turbine_events(country_filter=country)
    try:
        while True:
            event = await asyncio.wait_for(subscriber.get(), timeout=30.0)
            yield f'event: turbine_update\ndata: {json.dumps(event)}\n\n'
    except asyncio.TimeoutError:
        # Send heartbeat to keep the connection alive
        yield f': heartbeat\n\n'
    except asyncio.CancelledError:
        logger.info('sse_client_disconnected', country=country)
    finally:
        await subscriber.unsubscribe()

@app.get('/stream/turbines')
async def stream_turbine_updates(country: str | None = None):
    """SSE endpoint for real-time turbine status updates."""
    return StreamingResponse(
        turbine_event_stream(country=country),
        media_type='text/event-stream',
        headers={'Cache-Control': 'no-cache', 'X-Accel-Buffering': 'no'},
    )

The SSE endpoint opens a long-lived HTTP connection and yields events as they arrive from the internal event subscriber (backed by Redis Pub/Sub or Kafka). The country filter allows the Scottish operations team to subscribe only to Scottish turbine events, reducing bandwidth and processing overhead. The 30-second heartbeat prevents reverse proxies and load balancers from closing idle connections. When the client disconnects, the finally block unsubscribes from the event source to prevent resource leaks. At the Alliance, the SSE endpoint serves 8 concurrent dashboard connections (one per operations room across 3 countries, plus the Alliance-wide NOC), each receiving approximately 50-200 events per minute depending on grid conditions.
SSE vs WebSocket for Data Products

rows: Bidirectional (client can send messages to server)
Standard HTTP — works through all proxies, CDNs, and load balancers
Custom protocol upgrade — may be blocked by corporate proxies
Manual reconnection logic required in the client
Real-time dashboards, monitoring feeds, notification streams
Interactive applications: chat, collaborative editing, gaming
Not used: no data product requires bidirectional communication

blocks.content: The Alliance chose SSE over WebSocket because the turbine monitoring use case is strictly server-to-client: the dashboard receives updates but does not send data back to the server. SSE's advantages in this scenario are significant: automatic reconnection (the browser reconnects and resumes from the last event ID without application code), HTTP compatibility (SSE works through all corporate proxies and CDNs without special configuration), and simplicity (the server yields strings, the client uses the native EventSource API). WebSocket would add bidirectional capability that the use case does not need, at the cost of reconnection logic, proxy compatibility issues, and additional operational complexity for the WebSocket upgrade negotiation.

blocks.title: SSE connections are long-lived and must be bounded
blocks.content: Each SSE connection holds a server thread (or coroutine) and a subscription to the event source. Without connection limits, a misbehaving client that opens 1,000 connections can exhaust the server's resources. At the Alliance, the SSE endpoint enforces a per-team connection limit of 5 simultaneous connections (authenticated via the OAuth 2.0 token from lesson 6). Connections beyond the limit receive a 429 Too Many Requests response. The connection count is tracked as a Prometheus gauge and visualized on the API operations dashboard.

The Irish billing team's GraphQL query depends on the daily_kw field. The platform team renames it to consumption_kw to align with the Alliance's unified naming convention. If the rename is deployed without versioning, the billing team's query breaks: the field they request no longer exists. Versioning provides the migration window: the API serves both v1 (with daily_kw) and v2 (with consumption_kw) simultaneously. The billing team migrates to v2 at their own pace within the 90-day support window (Module 1 lesson 9). After 90 days, v1 is retired. The rename is a breaking change that does not break any consumer — because versioning absorbs the impact.
Versioning Strategies

rows: Clear and explicit — the version is visible in every request
Requires maintaining two separate schema definitions and resolvers
Used for major versions (v1 → v2) with breaking schema changes
No version in the URL — a single endpoint evolves over time
Consumers must check for deprecation warnings; easy to ignore
Used for minor changes (adding a field, deprecating a field)
Accept-Version: v2 header selects the schema version
Clean URL; version is explicit in the request
Easy to forget the header; debugging is harder than URL-based
Not used — URL-based is simpler for the Alliance's use cases

blocks.content: The Alliance uses a dual strategy: URL-based versioning for major breaking changes (field renames, type changes, removed fields) and GraphQL field deprecation for minor additive changes (new fields, new query arguments). A major version bump (v1 → v2) follows the platform release lifecycle from Module 1: 4-week preview, GA release, 90-day dual support, deprecation, and end-of-life. A field deprecation follows a simpler process: the field is marked @deprecated with a message pointing to the replacement, and the deprecation is logged in the consumer's response headers so monitoring can detect which teams still use deprecated fields. At the Alliance, major version bumps occur approximately twice per year; field deprecations occur monthly.
The Sunset Policy

Maintaining multiple API versions indefinitely is unsustainable: each version requires its own resolver logic, its own tests, its own documentation, and its own monitoring. The sunset policy limits this burden: v1 is retired 120 days after v2's GA release (90 days of dual support + 30 days of deprecation). After sunset, v1 requests return 410 Gone with a migration guide URL in the response body. At the Alliance, the API version dashboard tracks which teams have migrated to the latest version: a team still using v1 at day 60 receives a Slack notification, at day 80 an email to the team lead, and at day 100 an escalation to the engineering manager. These progressive reminders achieve 100% migration before sunset without requiring enforcement.

blocks.title: Versioning is a consumer relationship, not a technical mechanism
blocks.content: The URL prefix /v1/ is trivial to implement. The hard part is the organizational process: communicating the change, providing the migration guide, tracking adoption, and handling teams that cannot migrate within the window (a team in a deployment freeze, a team with a critical release that precludes API migration work). At the Alliance, the versioning process is owned by the data product's owner team, not by the platform team. The owner communicates with their consumers, writes the migration guide, and tracks adoption. The platform team provides the tooling (versioned routing, deprecation headers, adoption dashboard) but does not manage individual product migrations.

sections.title: Authentication and Authorization for Data Products

blocks.content: Module 5 mid-level secured secrets with environment variables and Azure Key Vault. Data product APIs require a different security model: machine-to-machine authentication (which service is calling the API?), team-level authorization (is this team permitted to read this data product?), and field-level authorization (is this team permitted to read the tariff_category field, which contains commercially sensitive pricing data?). The Alliance implements OAuth 2.0 with JWT tokens that encode the calling team's identity and scopes, providing authentication (who is calling) and authorization (what they are allowed to access) in a single token.
The OAuth 2.0 Machine-to-Machine Flow

The OAuth 2.0 client credentials flow: the consuming team's service requests a token from the Alliance's identity provider (Azure AD or Keycloak) with its client ID and secret. The identity provider returns a JWT containing the team's identity (sub: sc-billing), country (sc), and scopes (meters:read, meters:read:tariff). The service sends the JWT in the Authorization header with every API request. The API verifies the signature (the JWT was issued by the trusted identity provider), checks the expiry (tokens expire after 1 hour), and enforces the scopes (the sc-billing team has meters:read but not meters:write). At the Alliance, token issuance is managed by the platform team's identity provider; scope assignment is managed by the data product owner.
Field-Level Authorization

Some fields contain commercially sensitive data: tariff_category reveals pricing agreements, customer_address is PII subject to GDPR, and grid_frequency_hz is operationally sensitive for grid stability. Field-level authorization restricts which teams can query which fields. The meters:read scope provides access to the standard fields (meter_id, daily_kw, region, date). The meters:read:tariff scope adds access to tariff_category. The meters:read:pii scope adds access to customer_address (and is restricted to the team's own country's data for GDPR compliance). At the Alliance, field-level authorization is implemented in the GraphQL resolver: the resolver checks the JWT scopes before including restricted fields in the response, returning null for fields the consumer is not authorized to read.

blocks.title: Log every access denial as a security event
blocks.content: A 403 response is not just an error — it is a security signal. A team that repeatedly requests scopes it does not have may indicate a misconfigured client, a credential compromise, or an unauthorized access attempt. At the Alliance, every 403 response is logged as a security event with the team identity, the requested scope, the denied field, and the request timestamp. The security team reviews access denial patterns weekly. More than 10 denials from the same team in 24 hours triggers an automated investigation alert.

sections.title: Data Product SLAs and Rate Limits

blocks.content: Module 10 mid-level defined SLAs for pipeline freshness and completeness. Data product APIs add latency and throughput SLAs: the meter readings API must return p95 responses within 200 milliseconds, serve up to 1,000 requests per minute per team, and maintain 99.9% availability during business hours. These SLAs are commitments that the consuming teams depend on for their own pipelines' reliability: if the Scottish billing team's pipeline calls the Irish meter API and the API is down, the Scottish pipeline fails. The API's SLA must be stricter than its consumers' SLAs to prevent cascading failures.
Rate Limiting per Team

from fastapi import Request, HTTPException
import time
import redis

rate_limiter = redis.Redis(host='ratelimit.alliance.internal')

class TeamRateLimiter:
    """Token-bucket rate limiter per team."""
    def __init__(self, requests_per_minute: int = 1000):
        self.rpm = requests_per_minute

    async def check(self, team: str) -> None:
        key = f'ratelimit:{team}:{int(time.time()) // 60}'
        count = rate_limiter.incr(key)
        if count == 1:
            rate_limiter.expire(key, 120)  # expire after 2 minutes
        if count > self.rpm:
            retry_after = 60 - (int(time.time()) % 60)
            raise HTTPException(
                status_code=429,
                detail=f'Rate limit exceeded: {self.rpm} requests/minute',
                headers={'Retry-After': str(retry_after)},
            )

# Usage in the GraphQL endpoint
limiter = TeamRateLimiter(requests_per_minute=1000)

@app.post('/v1/graphql')
async def graphql_endpoint(request: Request, auth: dict = Depends(verify_token)):
    await limiter.check(auth['team'])
    # ... execute GraphQL query ...

The token-bucket rate limiter tracks request count per team per minute using Redis. When a team exceeds 1,000 requests in a minute, subsequent requests receive 429 Too Many Requests with a Retry-After header indicating when the limit resets. The rate limit is per team, not per client: all instances of the Scottish billing service share the sc-billing team's 1,000 RPM budget. At the Alliance, rate limits are tiered by SLA tier: platinum consumers (billing teams) get 2,000 RPM, gold consumers (analytics teams) get 1,000 RPM, and silver consumers (ad-hoc tools) get 200 RPM. The tier assignment is stored in the OAuth scope and enforced by the rate limiter.
The Circuit Breaker Pattern

When the data product's backend is overloaded (the Delta Lake table is being compacted, the Celery worker fleet is at capacity), continuing to accept requests degrades the experience for all consumers. The circuit breaker trips when the error rate exceeds 50% or the p95 latency exceeds 500 milliseconds: instead of attempting to serve the query (which would fail slowly), the API returns 503 Service Unavailable immediately, freeing server resources for recovery. The circuit breaker resets automatically after 30 seconds, allowing a test request through. If the test succeeds, the circuit closes and normal traffic resumes. At the Alliance, the circuit breaker tripped 3 times in the first quarter: twice during Delta Lake compaction (resolved by scheduling compaction outside query hours) and once during a Celery worker fleet crash (resolved in 2 minutes by HPA scale-up).

blocks.title: Expose the rate limit headers in every response, not just 429 responses
blocks.content: Consumers need to know their remaining budget to implement adaptive throttling: if 800 of 1,000 requests are consumed, the consumer can slow down proactively instead of hitting the limit and receiving errors. At the Alliance, every API response includes X-RateLimit-Limit (the budget), X-RateLimit-Remaining (the remaining budget), and X-RateLimit-Reset (the Unix timestamp when the budget resets). This transparency lets consuming teams build self-regulating clients that never hit the rate limit under normal conditions.

The meter readings API's p95 target is 200 milliseconds. A GraphQL query that reads from the Silver Delta Lake table, applies filters, and serializes the result takes 350 milliseconds cold — exceeding the SLA. Caching provides the missing 150 milliseconds: frequently requested queries are served from a Redis cache in 15 milliseconds, and only cold (never-seen) queries hit the storage layer. The challenge is cache invalidation: when the nightly Silver pipeline writes new data at 04:00, the cache must be invalidated so consumers see fresh data at 04:01, not yesterday's cached result.
The Tiered Caching Strategy

rows: Exact query match (hash of query + arguments)
Repeated identical queries within 5 minutes (dashboard auto-refresh)
Pre-computed results for common query patterns
Regional daily summaries that 4 teams query independently
All cache entries for the updated date partition
Nightly pipeline completion invalidates yesterday's cached results

blocks.content: The three tiers address different cache scenarios. The response cache handles identical queries from the same consumer within minutes (a dashboard that auto-refreshes every 30 seconds). The query result cache handles common patterns that multiple consumers query independently (4 teams all requesting DUBLIN-NORTH daily totals — the first query computes the result, the next 3 read from cache). Pipeline-triggered invalidation ensures freshness: when the Silver pipeline completes writing the 2025-01-15 partition, it sends a cache invalidation message that clears all cached results for that date. At the Alliance, the cache hit rate for the meter readings API averages 78%, reducing the effective p95 latency from 350 ms (cold) to 85 ms (blended with cache hits).
Cache Invalidation on Pipeline Completion

import redis
import structlog

logger = structlog.get_logger()
cache = redis.Redis(host='cache.alliance.internal')

def invalidate_cache_for_date(data_product: str, date: str) -> int:
    """Invalidate all cached responses for a specific date partition.

    Called by the pipeline's post-completion hook.
    Returns the number of cache entries deleted.
    """
    pattern = f'cache:{data_product}:*:date={date}:*'
    keys = list(cache.scan_iter(match=pattern, count=500))
    if keys:
        deleted = cache.delete(*keys)
        logger.info('cache_invalidated',
                    data_product=data_product, date=date, entries_deleted=deleted)
        return deleted
    return 0

# Called from the pipeline's lifecycle hook (Module 4 mid-level)
# after write_silver_idempotent completes:
#   invalidate_cache_for_date('ie.billing.meters', '2025-01-15')

The invalidation function scans for cache keys matching the data product and date, then deletes them in bulk. The SCAN command (not KEYS) avoids blocking Redis during the scan. At the Alliance, the post-completion hook (Module 4 mid-level's lifecycle hooks) calls the invalidation function immediately after the Silver write completes, ensuring that the next API query for that date reads fresh data. The invalidation adds approximately 50 milliseconds to the pipeline's completion time (scanning and deleting 30-50 cache entries), which is negligible relative to the pipeline's 63-second runtime.

blocks.title: Cache without invalidation is stale data served confidently
blocks.content: A cache that is never invalidated serves yesterday's data forever — and the consumer has no way to detect the staleness because the API returns 200 OK with valid-looking data. At the Alliance, every cached response includes an X-Cache-Age header showing how old the cached result is (in seconds). The consumer can detect stale data (X-Cache-Age: 86400 means the result is 24 hours old) and decide whether to accept it or request a cache bypass. The cache bypass header (Cache-Control: no-cache) forces a fresh query from the storage layer, bypassing all cache tiers.

sections.title: When NOT to Build a Data Product API

blocks.content: A data product API adds infrastructure (FastAPI server, Redis cache, rate limiter, OAuth integration), operational overhead (API monitoring, version management, SLA tracking), and latency (network round-trip, serialization, authentication). For the Irish billing team consuming Irish Silver data on the same Kubernetes cluster, the API adds 50 milliseconds of latency and 5 infrastructure components that a direct Parquet read does not require. The API is justified when consumers are remote (different cluster, different country), when access control must be field-level (PII restriction), or when multiple consumers benefit from caching and versioning. It is not justified when one team reads one dataset on the same server.
The API Decision Framework

rows: Consumer is in a different country or cluster
Build an API — direct file access is not possible
Multiple consumers (3+) query the same data product
Build an API — caching and versioning benefit all consumers
Field-level access control is required (PII, pricing)
Build an API — file-system permissions cannot restrict by column
Any data product with confidential or restricted fields
One consumer on the same cluster, no PII
Direct file access — the API overhead is unjustified
Single consumer with internal or public data classification
The data product serves ad-hoc exploratory queries
Provide a SQL endpoint (DuckDB, Trino) instead of a GraphQL API
Analytics teams doing exploratory analysis with unpredictable query patterns

blocks.content: At the Alliance, 18 data products have APIs (serving cross-country consumers, multiple teams, or PII-restricted data). The remaining 180+ data products are consumed via direct file access (team-internal consumption on the same cluster) or via the SQL endpoint (analytics teams running ad-hoc queries against Delta Lake tables via DuckDB). The 18 API-backed products account for approximately 40% of total data consumption by volume because they serve the highest-value, most-consumed datasets (meters, SCADA, prices). The 180+ direct-access products serve team-internal intermediate datasets that do not justify the API overhead.
The SQL Endpoint Alternative

Analytics teams write ad-hoc queries that change weekly: this week they aggregate by region, next week by customer type, the week after by time-of-day. A GraphQL schema cannot anticipate every possible aggregation. A SQL endpoint (DuckDB reading directly from Delta Lake, or Trino for federated queries) lets analysts write arbitrary SQL against the data product's storage without the API team defining every possible query shape. At the Alliance, the analytics SQL endpoint runs DuckDB in-process on the analyst's Jupyter notebook, reading directly from the Delta Lake tables on S3 with IAM-based access control. This provides the flexibility of SQL with the freshness of Delta Lake's transaction log, without any API server infrastructure.

blocks.title: The API is an investment, not a default
blocks.content: Building a data product API for a dataset consumed by one team on the same cluster is over-engineering: the API adds latency, infrastructure, and maintenance cost without providing any benefit that direct file access does not already provide. The API becomes valuable when the consumer base grows (caching amortizes across consumers), when consumers are remote (the API abstracts the storage location), or when access control requirements exceed file-system capabilities (field-level authorization). Build the API when the conditions justify it, not when the dataset is created.

sections.title: The Atlantic Energy Alliance Data Product Catalog

blocks.content: This module designed the API-first data product architecture that decouples producers from consumers: GraphQL for typed, consumer-driven queries with field-level selection (lesson 2), webhook endpoints for event-driven ingestion with at-least-once delivery and idempotent processing (lesson 3), Server-Sent Events for real-time streaming to operations dashboards (lesson 4), versioning with URL-based major versions and GraphQL field deprecation for minor changes (lesson 5), OAuth 2.0 with JWT-scoped field-level authorization (lesson 6), rate limiting with tiered budgets and circuit breaker protection (lesson 7), and tiered caching with pipeline-triggered invalidation for freshness guarantees (lesson 8).
The Data Product API Catalog

rows: 5 min response, 1h materialized, invalidated on pipeline completion
SSE: < 500 ms delivery; GraphQL: p95 < 300 ms
No SSE cache; 5 min GraphQL cache

blocks.content: The catalog records each data product's interface type, consumer list, SLA commitments, and caching strategy. It is the consumer-facing complement to the ownership registry from Module 1 (lesson 2): the ownership registry records who produces the data and who is accountable; the API catalog records how the data is consumed and what performance commitments exist. At the Alliance, the catalog is published as a Grafana dashboard with live metrics alongside the catalog entries: each product shows its current p95 latency, error rate, cache hit rate, and the number of active consumers. This live catalog replaces the static documentation that becomes stale within weeks of publication.

blocks.title: What comes next
blocks.content: Module 5 addresses the contract between producers and consumers at the schema level: what fields exist, what types they have, what changes are permitted, and how breaking changes are detected before they reach production. The GraphQL schema from this module defines the API surface; the Protobuf schema from Module 5 defines the data contract underneath. When the Irish billing team renames daily_kw to consumption_kw, Module 4's versioning manages the API migration, and Module 5's schema registry detects the breaking change in CI before it is deployed. The two modules together form the complete data product governance stack: Module 4 governs the consumption interface; Module 5 governs the data contract.

modules.title: Schema Registries and Data Contracts
modules.description: Design cross-organizational schema governance — Protobuf and Avro registries, automated breaking-change detection, contract testing, and the organizational workflows that coordinate schema changes across 10 teams and 3 countries.

blocks.content: At 3 teams in one country, schema changes were coordinated in a Slack message: 'We are renaming daily_kw to consumption_kw in the Silver meters schema on Friday.' The 2 consuming teams acknowledged, updated their code, and the rename shipped without incident. At 10 teams across 3 countries, the same rename broke 4 downstream consumers in Scotland and 2 in Brittany. The French billing team discovered the break at 07:00 when their pipeline produced zero rows — 4 hours after the Irish team deployed the change at 03:00. The Slack message was posted in the #ie-billing channel; the French and Scottish teams follow #fr-billing and #sc-billing. Nobody told them the schema was changing.
The Schema Governance Problem at Scale

rows: No registry of who consumes which schema fields
French and Scottish billing pipelines failed; 4 hours of stale data before detection
daily_kw changed from float64 to float32 — precision loss, not an error
Scottish analytics reported regional totals that were 0.02% lower than Irish totals for the same data
New required field breaks existing consumers
The producer added grid_zone as a non-nullable field; consumers that do not supply it get a validation error
3 analytics pipelines failed because they did not include grid_zone in their read schema
daily_kw means different things to different teams: gross production vs net after curtailment
The Alliance's cross-country grid report mixed gross and net values, producing nonsensical totals

blocks.content: Each failure is a coordination failure, not a technical failure. The registry does not track consumers (so the producer cannot notify them). The type change is invisible to file-based consumption (Parquet happily reads float32 into float64 with precision loss). The new field is a valid schema evolution that becomes a breaking change only because existing consumers do not expect it. The semantic ambiguity is undetectable by any automated tool. Schema governance addresses all four: the registry tracks producers and consumers, the compatibility checker detects breaking changes before deployment, the contract defines field semantics alongside field types, and the organizational process ensures cross-team coordination for schema changes.

blocks.title: Schema governance is the hardest non-technical problem in multi-team data engineering
blocks.content: The technical components (registry, compatibility checker, CI integration) are straightforward to implement. The organizational components (who approves schema changes, how breaking changes are negotiated, how the 90-day migration window is enforced) require cross-team coordination that tools alone cannot provide. This module covers both dimensions: the tools that detect and prevent schema breaks (lessons 2-5) and the organizational processes that coordinate schema evolution across 10 teams and 3 countries (lessons 6-8).

The Alliance needs a schema definition language that is language-neutral (Python pipelines, TypeScript dashboards, and Java analytics tools all consume the same data products), type-safe (field types are enforced at serialization, not at consumption time), and version-aware (adding, removing, and renaming fields follows explicit compatibility rules). Protocol Buffers (Protobuf) provides all three: a .proto file defines the schema with typed fields and field numbers, the protoc compiler generates Python classes for serialization and validation, and the field numbering system enables safe evolution (new fields get new numbers, removed fields reserve their old numbers).
The Meter Reading Proto Definition

Each field has a permanent number (meter_id = 1, consumption_kw = 2) that identifies it in the binary encoding. Field numbers are never reused: when raw_kw (field 100) was removed in v2, the number and name were reserved to prevent accidental reuse by a future developer. The optional keyword marks fields that may be absent (grid_zone exists for Irish meters but not for all Scottish meters). The reserved directive protects against two classes of error: reusing a field number (which causes deserialization corruption in consumers still on v1) and reusing a field name (which confuses developers who remember the old field's semantics). At the Alliance, the .proto files live in the platform monorepo (Module 1 lesson 3) under schemas/{namespace}/v{version}/.
Why Protobuf Over Avro for the Alliance

rows: By position or name depending on resolution mode
Protobuf: number-based identification prevents positional errors during evolution
Safe: reserve the number, consumers skip unknown fields
Requires a default value or union type for backward compatibility
Protobuf: simpler removal without default value complexity
~15% smaller wire size due to schema embedding
Protobuf: the Alliance's schemas are numeric-heavy (kW, MWh, Hz, temperatures)
Schema can be embedded in the data file
Protobuf: the Alliance already operates a schema registry; embedding is unnecessary
Strong in Hadoop, Kafka, Avro files
Protobuf: the Alliance's stack is API-first (Module 4), not Hadoop-based

blocks.content: The choice is not universal: Avro's schema embedding is advantageous when consumers do not have access to a registry, and Avro's union types provide more flexible nullable handling. The Alliance chose Protobuf because the API-first architecture (Module 4) already requires a schema registry for GraphQL schema management, Protobuf's field numbering prevents the positional errors that Avro's schema resolution can produce during rapid evolution, and the Alliance's numeric-heavy SCADA schemas benefit from Protobuf's faster serialization. Teams that integrate with Kafka-based external systems use Avro for those specific interfaces, with a Protobuf-to-Avro translation layer at the boundary.

blocks.title: Never reuse a Protobuf field number — ever
blocks.content: If field 100 was previously raw_kw (a float64) and is reused for source_system (a string), consumers on the old schema deserialize the string bytes as a float, producing a garbage value without an error. The reserved directive prevents this at the .proto level, and the Alliance's CI compatibility checker (lesson 5) enforces it: any PR that reuses a reserved field number fails the build. This rule is absolute — there is no exception process for reusing a field number.

A schema registry is a versioned catalog of schema definitions: every schema has a subject (the data product it describes), a version (monotonically increasing), and the schema definition itself (.proto file content). Producers register their schema before publishing data; consumers fetch the schema before reading data. The registry enforces compatibility rules: when a producer registers a new schema version, the registry checks whether the change is compatible with the previous version according to the configured compatibility mode. If the change is incompatible (a field was removed without reservation, a type was changed), the registration is rejected — preventing the breaking change from reaching the registry.
Registry Architecture

from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import datetime
import hashlib
import json
import structlog

logger = structlog.get_logger()

@dataclass(frozen=True)
class SchemaVersion:
    """A registered schema version in the Alliance schema registry."""
    subject: str              # alliance.ie.billing.meters
    version: int              # monotonically increasing
    schema_type: str          # protobuf | avro | json_schema
    schema_text: str          # the .proto or .avsc file content
    fingerprint: str          # SHA-256 of the normalized schema text
    compatibility: str        # BACKWARD | FORWARD | FULL | NONE
    registered_by: str        # ie-billing (team that registered this version)
    registered_at: datetime
    consumers: frozenset[str] # teams that have fetched this version

class SchemaRegistry:
    """The Alliance's schema registry with compatibility enforcement."""

    def register(
        self, subject: str, schema_text: str,
        schema_type: str = 'protobuf', compatibility: str = 'FULL',
    ) -> SchemaVersion:
        """Register a new schema version. Rejects incompatible changes."""
